narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a stack of numbers, but as the opening chapters of your story toward becoming a lawyer—and right now, those chapters have them worried about the final one: passing the bar exam. Your Year 3 decile of 3.0 stood out as the biggest red flag, signaling to them that even after some law school time, you were ranking in the lower third of your peers, which often foreshadows real struggles on the bar. That built on your Year 1 decile of 4.0, painting a picture of grades that started middling and dipped further, making them question if the rigors of legal study are clicking for you yet. Your LSAT score of 27 reinforced this, as it's on the lower side and suggests the analytical demands of the bar might be a steep climb. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer of concern, since students in similar situations sometimes lack the extra resources—like tutors or prep courses—that can make a difference. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 echoed these academic hurdles from the start, while being a white male in a part-time program didn't shift the tide much, as those traits have historically aligned with tougher odds in this prediction. Together, these pieces formed a profile that our model sees as carrying a 41% chance of bar failure—enough risk that they had to say no to protect their program's success rate. It's tough to hear, especially when you're passionate about law, but this is their way of spotting patterns from thousands of journeys like yours. The good news? You're early in this—focusing on boosting those deciles or retaking the LSAT could rewrite your story entirely. You've got the drive; now it's about stacking the odds in your favor. Hang in there—we believe in second acts.
